ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised pig trotter; mapped to broader pork food product class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for braised pig trotter; A00LC (pig meat and offal, cooked) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for braised pig trotter; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of braised pig trotter is approximately 400 g (1 bowl), which includes bone-in trotter pieces with braising sauce, as commonly portioned in Malaysian-Chinese hawker stalls and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork, fresh, feet, raw (FDC ID 168186) and Pork, cured, feet, pickled (FDC ID 174621), adapted for braised preparation with soy sauce and sugar
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Malaysian-style braised pig trotter exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of April 2026. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central pork feet entries (FDC IDs 168186 and 174621), adjusted upward for sodium contribution from dark and light soy sauce braising liquid, and for sugar from rock sugar. The input record values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 11.25 g, fat 11.25 g, carbohydrate 3.75 g, sodium 450 mg per 100 g) are consistent with USDA pork feet cooked data adjusted for braising sauce absorption and are retained as the best available estimate. Saturated fat 4.5 g, dietary fibre 0.25 g, sugar 2.0 g, cholesterol 45 mg, calcium 10 mg, iron 0.75 mg, trans fat 0.13 g, and vitamin C 0.0 mg are also consistent with USDA reference data for cooked pork feet with soy-based sauce.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate based on braised pork with soy sauce and minimal carbohydrate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Malaysian braised pig trotter. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous braised pork dishes with soy sauce and rock sugar from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with only 3.75 g carbohydrate per 100 g, resulting in a very low glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: GI (30) × carbohydrate per serving (15 g from 400 g portion) / 100 = 4.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold, driven by dark and light soy sauce in the braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content of 450 mg per 100 g; if consumed, request reduced soy sauce preparation, avoid drinking the braising liquid, and restrict portion size to no more than 100–150 g per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 3.75 g per 100 g and low sugar at 2.0 g per 100 g; high protein slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised pig trotter has minimal glycaemic impact due to its very low carbohydrate content (3.75 g/100 g) and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, though the high fat content (11.25 g/100 g) and caloric density warrant portion control and pairing with high-fibre vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.5 g per 100 g, cholesterol 45 mg per 100 g, trans fat 0.13 g per 100 g, and sodium 450 mg per 100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume braised pig trotter sparingly due to its combined burden of saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g), cholesterol (45 mg/100 g), trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), and high sodium (450 mg/100 g), all of which contribute to atherogenic and hypertensive risk; portion size should not exceed 100 g and frequency should be limited to occasional consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is high in arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); sodium 450 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit braised pig trotter due to its pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content from pork and high sodium from soy sauce; if consumed, request reduced soy sauce preparation and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as ginger and omega-3-rich vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Pig Trotter (卤猪脚, Lǔ Zhū Jiǎo) is a classic Malaysian-Chinese slow-braised dish in which whole or halved pig trotters are simmered for several hours in a rich, dark, aromatic braising liquid made from dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, rock sugar, cinnamon, star anise, cloves, ginger, and garlic. The prolonged braising renders the collagen-rich skin and connective tissue gelatinous and tender, while the meat absorbs the deeply savoury, slightly sweet, and spiced flavour of the sauce. The dish is commonly served over steamed white rice or with braised tofu and hard-boiled eggs in the same braising liquid. It is a staple of Malaysian-Chinese hawker culture and is widely available in Singapore food courts and via food delivery platforms. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 11.25 g protein, 11.25 g fat (of which 4.5 g is saturated), 3.75 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g sugar, 0.25 g dietary fibre, and 450 mg sodium. The high sodium content from soy sauce makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease unless prepared with reduced-sodium soy sauce. The low carbohydrate content and high protein make it glycaemically benign for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. The collagen content from the trotter skin may support joint health, though the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid in pork and high sodium are concerns for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Malaysian-style braised pig trotter was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of April 2026. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central pork feet entries (FDC IDs 168186 and 174621) adjusted for braising sauce sodium and sugar absorption. The input record values are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained. Confidence is medium because the values are derived from an analogous USDA source rather than a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for braised pig trotter is not available in USDA FoodData Central pork feet entries or Singapore HPB database at the dish level as of April 2026. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reliably available for this specific braised preparation in any approved source consulted as of April 2026; USDA FoodData Central pork feet entries do not provide complete micronutrient panels for the cooked braised form. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed as effectively zero for a pork-based braised dish with no significant vitamin C-contributing ingredients; consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked pork feet. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on braised pork with soy sauce and minimal carbohydrate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as supporting only menu_listing and was not used as a nutrient evidence source; USDA FoodData Central was used instead as the approved primary source. 6. The dish is not halal as it contains pork.
